title.en: Cross-Cloud Networking: Interconnects, VPNs & Latency Reality

description.en: Connect Azure, AWS, and GCP estates with VPNs, dedicated interconnects, and carrier fabrics — and learn to budget latency and egress before physics and finance do it for you.

story_variants.en:
## The Demo That Died at 80 Milliseconds

A team demos a slick architecture: the web tier on AKS in Azure, the recommendation engine on SageMaker in AWS, the feature store on BigQuery in GCP. It works beautifully in the demo — everything deployed in one metro area. In production, the feature store lands in a different continent for data-residency reasons, every page load makes four cross-cloud calls, and p95 latency explodes. Nobody changed the code. Physics changed the deployment.

Cross-cloud networking is where multi-cloud strategy meets the speed of light, and the speed of light does not negotiate.

## Your Connectivity Options, Worst to Best

- **Public internet + TLS.** Fine for low-volume, latency-tolerant API calls. Unpredictable jitter, no bandwidth guarantees.
- **Site-to-site VPN between clouds.** IPsec tunnels between Azure VPN Gateway, AWS Site-to-Site VPN, and GCP Cloud VPN endpoints. Cheap, quick to stand up, encrypted — but throughput per tunnel is bounded (roughly single-digit Gbps with tricks like tunnel ECMP), and you still ride the internet or provider peering underneath.
- **Dedicated circuits via a colocation/carrier fabric.** The enterprise-grade answer: Azure **ExpressRoute**, AWS **Direct Connect**, and GCP **Cloud Interconnect** each terminate in carrier-neutral facilities (Equinix, Digital Realty, Megaport POPs). A software-defined fabric (Equinix Fabric, Megaport, Console Connect) cross-connects them, giving you private, SLA-backed paths *between* clouds without owning routers in a cage — though owning your own edge routers remains an option at scale.
- **Cloud-native multicloud products.** Offerings like Google's Cross-Cloud Interconnect provide managed private links from GCP directly toward other clouds, shrinking the DIY surface.

| Concern | Azure | AWS | GCP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dedicated circuit | ExpressRoute | Direct Connect | Cloud Interconnect (incl. Cross-Cloud Interconnect) |
| Managed VPN | VPN Gateway | Site-to-Site VPN | Cloud VPN (HA VPN) |
| Transit hub | Virtual WAN / hub VNet | Transit Gateway | Network Connectivity Center |
| Private service access | Private Link / Private Endpoint | PrivateLink | Private Service Connect |
| Global backbone behavior | Microsoft global network | AWS Global Accelerator / backbone | Premium Tier routing |

> **Callout:** The transit-hub row matters most for architecture drawings: hub-and-spoke VNets, Transit Gateway attachments, and NCC hubs are the three dialects of "one place where routes meet." Your cross-cloud link should land in these hubs — never spoke-to-spoke.

## Latency Budgets: Do the Arithmetic First

Rules of thumb every principal architect carries:

- Same metro, cloud-to-cloud over a fabric: **~1-3 ms**.
- Same continent, different regions: **~10-40 ms** each way.
- Transatlantic: **~70-90 ms**. Transpacific: often **100+ ms**.
- A synchronous user request that fans out across clouds pays the worst path *per call*, multiplied by call count.

Build a **latency budget** like a cost budget: allocate milliseconds to each hop of the critical path, sum them, and compare against the SLO *before* committing the architecture. If the sum breaches the budget, your options are: colocate the chatty components, replace synchronous calls with async replication, or cache aggressively at the boundary.

> **Callout:** Egress pricing has the same shape as latency: crossing a cloud boundary costs more than staying inside one. Even after 2024-era reforms (all three providers waive egress for customers *fully leaving* a platform), steady-state inter-cloud traffic remains billable. Design data flows as if every gigabyte across the boundary must justify itself — because on the invoice, it will.

## Design Patterns That Work

- **Coarse-grained boundaries.** Cross the cloud boundary once per request (or zero times), not four. APIs between clouds should be batch-y and asynchronous where possible.
- **Replicate reads, centralize writes.** Keep a read replica or curated extract in the consuming cloud; ship changes asynchronously.
- **Terminate in hubs, inspect at the edge.** Firewalls/inspection at the hub in each cloud; the inter-cloud link itself stays a clean routed path.
- **Non-overlapping IP plan.** The most boring and most violated rule: a global IPAM covering all three clouds. Overlapping CIDRs discovered *after* interconnect provisioning force NAT contortions forever.
- **Redundancy in the physical layer.** Two circuits into two different POPs/edge locations. A single interconnect through a single facility is a scheduled outage you have not scheduled yet.

## Real Failure Modes

- **The 10.0.0.0/16 collision.** Azure and AWS estates built independently, both squatting the same CIDR. Interconnect project stalls a quarter for re-IP or double-NAT.
- **Asymmetric routing.** Traffic exits via the fabric but returns via VPN (or the internet) because route priorities differ per side; stateful firewalls drop the flow intermittently. Debugging this consumes weeks without flow logs on both ends.
- **The single-tunnel VPN in production.** Works for months, then a maintenance event drops the tunnel and takes the "resilient multi-cloud architecture" with it.
- **MTU mismatches.** Encapsulation overhead pushes packets past the path MTU; large payloads fail while health checks (small packets) pass. Symptoms look like application bugs.

## What You Will Build

In the challenge you will implement the latency-budget calculator: sum per-hop latencies along named cross-cloud paths and flag SLA breaches — the two-minute arithmetic that, done early, prevents the 80 ms demo funeral described above.

description.en: Dedicated circuits from each cloud meet in a carrier-neutral fabric; links land in each cloud's transit hub; every path is checked against a latency budget.

left.label: Chatty synchronous calls across clouds on every user request

right.label: Coarse boundaries: replicate reads, async writes, cross once or never

description.en: Each synchronous cross-cloud hop pays latency and egress per call. Coarse, asynchronous boundaries pay once.
A private cross-cloud path end to end — every stage adds milliseconds that belong in the budget.

explanation.en: A path is a list of (hop_name, milliseconds) — model it before you build it.
Latency is additive along the critical path; sum every hop, including the fabric cross-connect.
Compare against the SLO the business signed, not against 'feels fast in the demo'.

description.en: The transatlantic path breaches its budget — caught in review, not in production.

instruction.en: Build the latency budget checker architects run before approving a cross-cloud design. Implement path_latency(path) returning the integer sum of hop latencies (path['hops'] is a list of (name, ms) tuples) and verdict(path) returning 'OK' if the total is <= path['sla_ms'] else 'BREACH'. For each path in PATHS (in order) print exactly '<name>: <total>ms (sla <sla>ms) -> <verdict>'. Then print 'Breaches: <n>/<total>'. Doing this arithmetic on paper is how you stop physics from killing the architecture in production.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
PATHS = [
    {"name": "azure-eu-to-aws-eu", "hops": [("app-gw", 2), ("fabric-crossconnect", 8), ("target-lb", 2)], "sla_ms": 20},
    {"name": "azure-eu-to-gcp-us", "hops": [("app-gw", 2), ("transatlantic", 78), ("target-lb", 3)], "sla_ms": 50},
    {"name": "aws-us-to-gcp-us", "hops": [("nlb", 1), ("fabric-crossconnect", 12), ("target-lb", 2)], "sla_ms": 25},
]

def path_latency(path):
    # TODO: sum the ms of every hop
    pass

def verdict(path):
    # TODO: 'OK' if total <= sla_ms else 'BREACH'
    pass

# TODO: print '<name>: <total>ms (sla <sla>ms) -> <verdict>' per path
# TODO: print 'Breaches: <n>/<total>'
practice_challenges.expected_output:
azure-eu-to-aws-eu: 12ms (sla 20ms) -> OK
azure-eu-to-gcp-us: 83ms (sla 50ms) -> BREACH
aws-us-to-gcp-us: 15ms (sla 25ms) -> OK
Breaches: 1/3

en: path_latency: sum(ms for _, ms in path['hops']).
verdict calls path_latency and compares with <= against path['sla_ms'] — equal to the SLA still counts as OK.
Format each line with an f-string: f"{p['name']}: {total}ms (sla {p['sla_ms']}ms) -> {v}" and count BREACH results for the summary.

practice_challenges.test_code:
assert path_latency({"hops": [("a", 5), ("b", 7)], "sla_ms": 99}) == 12
assert path_latency({"hops": [], "sla_ms": 1}) == 0
assert verdict({"hops": [("a", 10), ("b", 10)], "sla_ms": 20}) == "OK"
assert verdict({"hops": [("a", 10), ("b", 11)], "sla_ms": 20}) == "BREACH"
assert verdict({"hops": [("a", 1)], "sla_ms": 1}) == "OK"

q.en: Which trio names the dedicated private circuit products of Azure, AWS, and GCP respectively?

en: Private Link, PrivateLink, Private Service Connect
VPN Gateway, Transit Gateway, Cloud Router

q.en: Why do carrier-neutral facilities (Equinix, Megaport POPs) matter for multi-cloud networking?

en: They provide free bandwidth between clouds
All three providers' dedicated circuits terminate there, so a fabric can cross-connect them privately
They replace the need for IP address planning

q.en: A cross-cloud flow works, then intermittently drops through the stateful firewall. Route tables show traffic leaving via the fabric but returning via VPN. What is this failure mode called?
What is the most effective design response when a synchronous cross-cloud call chain breaches its latency budget?

en: Increase the SLA number until it passes
Colocate the chatty components or replace sync calls with async replication and caching
Switch all traffic to the public internet
